jokes.text: I packed my alarm! / No bra! Save time!
jokes.setup: Abbi has overslept and is frantically explaining why she can't just turn off an alarm
jokes.punchline: She packed her alarm (nonsensical—how does one pack an alarm?) and then declares she's skipping a bra purely for time savings, treating bra-wearing as a significant time cost
jokes.explanation: Two quick jokes stacked efficiently. 'I packed my alarm' is delightfully absurd—the logic doesn't hold up but it's delivered with frantic sincerity. 'No bra, save time' is a sharp comic beat about the very real female experience of bra-as-burden, elevated by being treated as legitimate time strategy.

jokes.text: It's 10:44. / Okay, we got this.
jokes.setup: Abbi panics about being late at 10:04; Ilana corrects her to 10:44 — they're even later than panicked Abbi thought
jokes.punchline: Abbi responds with unearned confidence: 'Okay, we got this'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 10:04 to 10:44 turns a bad situation worse in a single beat, and 'we got this' as a response to genuinely worsened odds is a clean ironic pivot. Short and punchy, which is its main strength.

jokes.text: They arrive at the bodega to discover it's literally gone — just a vacant lot or closed storefront
jokes.setup: The entire cold open has been an urgent race through New York to reach 'the best bacon, egg, and cheese in New York City'
jokes.punchline: The bodega simply no longer exists — the quest is over before it begins
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom misdirection executed cleanly: the entire cold open setup—the alarm, the no-bra, the crowd-parting—is a windup that delivers nothing. The wordless visual of the missing bodega is the punchline. The 'Oh no!' reaction beat lands it. Structurally elegant.

jokes.text: We're gonna get one right now and forget that ever happened when we're taking painful bacon, egg, and cheese shits later, okay?
jokes.setup: They've just lost their beloved bodega; Ilana offers consolation
jokes.punchline: The consolation is graphic digestive prophecy — she promises painful diarrhea as the silver lining
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Ilana — she turns comfort into digestive prophecy without missing a beat. The sincere 'okay?' at the end, as if this is a normal thing to promise a friend, is what makes it character comedy rather than just gross-out. Abbi's 'Oh, I can't wait' sells it further.

jokes.text: I know it's 'wate,' but I really would like some 'bweakfast' food.
jokes.setup: They enter a new deli and need to charm the staff into making eggs after 11am
jokes.punchline: Ilana performs a baby-talk affectation — 'wate' for 'late' and 'bweakfast' — apparently as a manipulation strategy
jokes.explanation: The baby lisp as a deliberate charm tactic is bizarre and specific in the best way. Ilana deploying infantile helplessness as a negotiation strategy is consistent with her chaotic interpersonal approach. The word 'wate' especially lands because it's just wrong enough.

jokes.text: This is this angel's final breakfast in New York City before she goes and rebuilds homes for Christian tsunami victims.
jokes.setup: The deli worker says no eggs after 11; Ilana pivots to an elaborate fabricated emotional appeal
jokes.punchline: She claims Abbi is about to leave to help 'Christian tsunami victims' — an absurdly specific fake charity framing
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Christian tsunami victims' — not just 'tsunami victims,' not just 'Christian victims,' but the intersection — is sharp comic writing. It sounds real enough to almost work while being absurd. 'Okay, that's not true' from Abbi is the perfect straight-man response.

jokes.text: You are gonna deny her this Make-A-Wish? For shame. Shame!
jokes.setup: Ilana's fake charity story isn't working; she escalates to a Make-A-Wish framing and then a moral shaming
jokes.punchline: 'Shame!' — the single-word verdict delivered to a deli worker over eggs
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'tsunami victims' to 'Make-A-Wish' to 'SHAME' is perfectly paced. 'Shame!' as a standalone exclamation — delivered like the Game of Thrones scene — is quotable and absurdly disproportionate to the stakes of a breakfast sandwich.

jokes.text: I'm just gonna leave some money for you fellas. / That was inappropriate.
jokes.setup: After the 'Shame!' speech, Ilana apparently leaves cash — possibly as payment, possibly as an apology bribe
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat 'That was inappropriate' — as if the money-leaving is the problem, not the entire preceding spectacle
jokes.explanation: Abbi's underreaction — calmly categorizing the money-leaving as 'inappropriate' after a full Make-A-Wish sermon — is a well-executed straight-man beat. The mismatch of offense level is the joke. Light but clean.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no. We're not doing this. [cab rejection visual gag]
jokes.setup: They're now trying to leave with food (or heading somewhere else) and apparently attempt to hail a cab with some complication
jokes.punchline: The rejection — presumably a cab won't stop or something blocks them — is met with Abbi's weary 'Oh no no no' as a kneejerk refusal to engage
jokes.explanation: Without the visual, this is thin — it reads as a transitional beat more than a constructed joke. The dialogue suggests a visual gag (cab won't take them, possibly with the toilet already in play), but the transcript doesn't give enough to fully assess. Mid-range by necessity.

jokes.text: What does Colorado have to offer that New York City doesn't? / Well, for one, recreational weed.
jokes.setup: Ilana is emotionally resisting Abbi's move by listing New York's superiority — culture, energy, identity
jokes.punchline: Abbi's simple, practical answer undercuts the whole romantic speech: legal weed
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect comedic structure: a long emotional rhetorical question deflated in four words. The beat after ('Right.') — Ilana unable to argue with this — is exactly right. Abbi's delivery of practical truth against Ilana's romantic attachment to NYC is a great character contrast moment. The joke is slightly less original in 2019 Colorado-weed territory but the structure earns it.

jokes.text: Abbi, this is a Wa-Unchi toilet. Don't you see? We thought we were on some classic Abbi and Ilana New York City journey today. We thought it was for the bacon, egg, and cheese. When, in fact, this was our mission all along.
jokes.setup: They've stumbled upon an abandoned high-tech toilet on the street
jokes.punchline: Ilana reframes the entire day's misadventure as having been cosmically destined to find this toilet — with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: This earns its high score on character integration alone — only Ilana Glazer's character would (1) find a discarded luxury toilet, (2) immediately identify its brand name, and (3) deliver a full philosophical reframe of the day's events around it with zero irony. The callback to the failed bacon-egg-and-cheese quest is structurally elegant. It also perfectly captures the show's DNA: every mundane errand becomes a cosmic journey. The earnestness is what sells it — she's not joking.

jokes.text: It's an intelligent toilet with a UV light that breaks down the germs of fecal matter in the bowl. It has a heated seat and warm water that sprays in your ass after you take a dump just like a bidet. God, every time you are sprayed, you are reborn.
jokes.setup: Abbi is skeptical of the toilet scheme; Ilana explains its features
jokes.punchline: The product description climaxes with 'every time you are sprayed, you are reborn' — a spiritual awakening framed around a bidet function
jokes.explanation: The escalation from technical product features to spiritual transcendence is perfectly Ilana. 'Every time you are sprayed, you are reborn' is genuinely quotable — it's the kind of line that could be stitched on a pillow ironically. The delivery of 'just like a bidet' as though clarifying for Abbi's benefit and then immediately going transcendent is excellent comic rhythm.

jokes.text: Come on. It's a $10,000 toilet. / Wow. / I need this.
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to convince Abbi to help steal/transport a discarded toilet
jokes.punchline: The price tag — $10,000 — lands Abbi into genuine awe, followed by Ilana's simple 'I need this' as if that settles any debate
jokes.explanation: 'I need this' as the complete justification for hauling a $10,000 toilet across Brooklyn is funny in its economy. Abbi's 'Wow' — momentarily won over by the price — suggests they're both broke women suddenly taking this toilet very seriously as an asset.

jokes.text: You won't regret it, mister! [shouted to no one as they begin carrying the toilet]
jokes.setup: They decide to take the toilet and begin maneuvering it
jokes.punchline: Ilana reassures an absent 'mister' — the toilet's previous owner presumably — as if seeking permission from a ghost
jokes.explanation: A small, well-placed bit — Ilana's social instinct to reassure even an absent party about a decision is consistent character behavior. It's quick and doesn't overstay.

jokes.text: Toilet discrimination.
jokes.setup: A cab or Uber refuses to take them because they have a toilet
jokes.punchline: Ilana identifies this as a civil rights issue: 'toilet discrimination'
jokes.explanation: Two words. Maximum economy. The joke is that Ilana's instinct — honed to identify discrimination in the real world — fires on this absurd situation. It's character-specific, quotable, and perfectly timed. The joke works because Ilana is the kind of person who WOULD frame this as discrimination, and the show has earned that character consistency.

jokes.text: Uber is surging 60 times the normal rate. / Woof, my rating is very low. / Probably from the pee incident. Again, sorry about that. / Totally fine.
jokes.setup: They need to get the toilet home; Abbi checks Uber
jokes.punchline: Ilana's low rating, traced back to a 'pee incident' that is not explained — and Abbi's 'Totally fine' suggests she was present and has somehow accepted this
jokes.explanation: The 'pee incident' is never elaborated upon and doesn't need to be — the joke is entirely in the casual delivery and Abbi's resigned acceptance. 'Totally fine' from Abbi, who presumably was involved or victimized, is perfectly Abbi: she's processed it and moved on. The unexplained backstory is funnier than any explanation could be.

jokes.text: We're gonna walk it across the bridge? / That's right. / Kids are suckers, man. 70 bucks for an old skateboard? Too easy.
jokes.setup: They need to transport a $10,000 toilet to Brooklyn with no viable vehicle options
jokes.punchline: Their solution: walk it across the Brooklyn Bridge on a skateboard purchased by scamming a child
jokes.explanation: The confident 'Kids are suckers, man' — delivered with no guilt about exploiting a child for their toilet heist — is dark and perfectly Ilana. The $70 price point for 'an old skateboard' suggests Ilana either overpaid or thinks $70 is nothing, both of which are funny. The entire logistics pivot is absurdist but has its own internal logic.

jokes.text: Ilana, I'm really proud of you, dude. I mean, obviously, for going back to school to become the most badass therapist New York City has ever seen, but also for the Phone Wigs! I mean, I really feel like you have something there. Like, that has legs, you know?
jokes.setup: Abbi sincerely lists things she's proud of Ilana for
jokes.punchline: She puts 'Phone Wigs' — a presumably absurd business idea — on equal or near-equal footing with going back to school for a graduate degree
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Abbi's earnestness — she genuinely believes in Phone Wigs. 'That has legs, you know?' applied to wigs for phones is a perfect absurdist touch. The sincerity is what sells it: Abbi isn't being ironic, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I strongly, strongly believe in the joint gynecologist/waxing/laser hair removal thing. It's like a one-stop shop. Come on, that's genius.
jokes.setup: They're listing their shared business ideas
jokes.punchline: A gynecology/waxing/laser combo one-stop shop — medicalized and groomed simultaneously
jokes.explanation: This reads like an actual idea Ilana Glazer herself once pitched in a writer's room. The logic is almost sound — efficiency! — which makes it funnier than pure absurdism. The enthusiasm ('Come on, that's genius') is crucial; she's not winking at the audience.

jokes.text: I still also believe in the Dorito dust. I mean, I'm just eating the chip for the dust. / Exactly. / And it's like cut out the middleman and get me a vial straight of that dust for spicing or for swigging.
jokes.setup: Listing more business ideas
jokes.punchline: A concentrated Dorito dust delivery system — 'for spicing or for swigging' — treating dust like a pharmaceutical or a condiment in vial form
jokes.explanation: 'For spicing or for swigging' is genuinely funny — the two uses are so different in class that putting them in the same phrase is perfect. Abbi's 'Exactly' as enthusiastic co-sign suggests this is a shared truth they've arrived at independently. The product concept has an almost-real-world logic that makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I just found out, dude, that Doritos are tortilla chips. / Wild. / I mean, no one talks about that.
jokes.setup: They've been discussing Dorito dust as a business concept
jokes.punchline: Abbi has apparently recently discovered that Doritos are, in fact, tortilla chips — delivered as breaking news — and Ilana validates this as suppressed information
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. The premise — that Abbi, a grown adult, has only recently learned that Doritos are tortilla chips — is perfectly absurd, but the delivery is what elevates it. 'I mean, no one talks about that' from Ilana, treating it as genuinely obscured information rather than printed on every bag, is perfect deadpan validation of stupidity. Two extremely funny women at the top of their rapport. It's economical, character-specific (only these two would have this conversation this sincerely), and quotably bizarre.

jokes.text: Ilana's full apocalypse preparedness speech: banks hacked, hidden nooks, pee straws, zip-off pants, contact lenses, Lasik
jokes.setup: Ilana pivots from friendship concerns about Abbi moving away to apocalypse logistics
jokes.punchline: A fully-developed survival plan that includes 'straws that allow you to drink your own pee' and zip-off pants delivered with the gravity of genuine emergency planning
jokes.explanation: The sustained apocalypse planning sequence is a great example of Broad City's escalation comedy — each item in the list is funnier because it's delivered with equal weight. The pee straws alongside the Lasik suggestion ('Good call. Good call.') creates the sense that these are all equally valid preparation steps. The sequence also works emotionally: Ilana's apocalypse anxiety is really about separation anxiety, which the show earns.

jokes.text: Colorado is where I thought Montana was. / You're gonna be actually where I thought Oklahoma was. / That's cool. Mormons are dope.
jokes.setup: They're looking at a map to plan their apocalypse meeting point
jokes.punchline: Ilana's geographic ignorance is total — she's confused all four states — and concludes with a non-sequitur endorsement of Mormons who aren't where she thinks they are
jokes.explanation: Ilana's geographic confusion as a running character trait is used perfectly here — she doesn't know where any of these states are and doesn't care. 'Mormons are dope' as a segue from geographic error is perfectly Ilana: generous, enthusiastic, and completely misplaced. Abbi's gentle correction and 'Momo's still dope, though' from Ilana is a nice tag.

jokes.text: Well, I think Utah is actually the Mormon area. / Oh, right, right, right. / Momo's still dope, though. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Ilana has praised Mormons for being in Colorado (they aren't)
jokes.punchline: Corrected, Ilana immediately transfers her enthusiasm to 'Momo's' — apparently what she calls Mormons — without missing a beat
jokes.explanation: 'Momo's still dope, though' is a small perfect tag — the nickname alone is funny, and the fact that the endorsement survives factual correction is consistent character behavior. Quick and well-executed.

jokes.text: So, should we, uh... should we jump? / Yeah. / No, seriously, should we jump? / We can't. We have our big dinner date tonight. / You said the reservations were, like, really hard to get.
jokes.setup: They've stopped on the Brooklyn Bridge in an emotional moment about Abbi's departure
jokes.punchline: The only reason not to jump off the Brooklyn Bridge is the dinner reservation — delivered without irony, with the reservation treated as a genuine competing concern
jokes.explanation: This is the second-best joke of the episode. The setup is ambiguous enough (are they being poetic? are they serious?) that the punchline — 'We can't. We have our big dinner date tonight' — lands like a cold shower of perfect mundane pragmatism. The reservation as the only thing standing between them and a bridge jump is both funny and somehow deeply true to NYC priorities. The specificity of 'really hard to get' adds the final layer. Dark, sharp, and exactly calibrated to these characters' rapport.

jokes.text: Bevers on the roof — sunburned nude everywhere, locked out, needs his kimono
jokes.setup: Abbi returns home; Bevers calls from the roof
jokes.punchline: He was sunbathing nude on the roof again, is now 'extremely sunburned. Everywhere.' and needs the kimono he apparently keeps for this purpose
jokes.explanation: Bevers as the gross, boundary-free male roommate is a running character gag, and this is a solid deployment — the 'again' implies habitual nude rooftop sunbathing, the specificity of a kimono as emergency coverage, and 'Everywhere' as the sunburn descriptor earns a good laugh. The setup is thin but the execution is sharp.

jokes.text: All right, Bevers, uh, you know, cover yourself up. I don't want to see your sunburned penis. / Open your eyes. / No. / It's kosher, dude. Open 'em up. / Surprise!
jokes.setup: Abbi navigates the nude Bevers situation with eyes presumably closed; the scene appears to be Bevers alone
jokes.punchline: Ilana is there — it's a surprise party setup — and the punchline of the whole Bevers nude call was a misdirection to get Abbi up to the roof
jokes.explanation: The Bevers-nude setup as a misdirection device for the surprise party reveal is clever structural comedy — the most disgusting possible setup leads to a genuine warm surprise. 'It's kosher, dude. Open 'em up' from Bevers — apparently knowing the plan — followed by Ilana's 'Surprise!' is a well-built reversal. It also pays off the Bevers character beat economically.

jokes.text: Surprise! [Ilana reveals she's set up a rooftop farewell party instead of going home]
jokes.setup: Ilana appeared to say her final goodbye on the bridge; Abbi is now closing her eyes on the roof expecting only Bevers
jokes.punchline: Ilana has staged an elaborate rooftop surprise party — the 'I can't say goodbye again' bridge moment was a cover so she could set this up
jokes.explanation: A structurally satisfying misdirection that works emotionally AND comedically. The Bevers sunburn call was a plant to get Abbi to the roof with eyes closed. The entire bridge goodbye was a fake-out. On rewatch, everything — Ilana's 'I can't say goodbye again,' Bevers's convenient nudity — reads as deliberate. Very high rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Trey gives Abbi free weights as a going-away gift. / Thank you so much. I'll... I'll try to put it on my carry-on.
jokes.setup: Trey, Abbi's gym-obsessed ex-hookup, gives her a going-away present
jokes.punchline: It's free weights — an extremely heavy, impractical gift for someone flying across the country — and Abbi's polite attempt to find a positive spin ('I'll try to put it on my carry-on') is perfectly funny in its futility
jokes.explanation: Character integration is the strength here — only Trey would give free weights as a parting gift, and only Abbi would politely pretend to find a way to make it work. The idea of free weights in a carry-on is physically absurd enough to get a big laugh.

jokes.text: I've always said, Abbi and incremental free weights, match made in heaven.
jokes.setup: Trey has given Abbi free weights; she's being politely diplomatic about it
jokes.punchline: He has apparently said this before — 'I've always said' — as though it's a well-established truth about Abbi's destiny with beginner weights
jokes.explanation: 'I've always said' applied to something this specific and absurd is perfectly Trey — the character believes in his own gym wisdom with total sincerity. 'Incremental' is a funny word choice that somehow makes it worse.

jokes.text: Abbi Gary Anna Abrams, will you marry me? / Um... oh, Trey. / Two carats. / No. Right? / Right, yeah, no. / Yeah, no.
jokes.setup: Trey says it's 'now or never' at Abbi's going-away party
jokes.punchline: He proposes with a full name invocation — including 'Gary Anna' as middle names — and then immediately validates her 'no' as if he expected it and they both acknowledge the mutual absurdity in overlapping 'no's and 'yeah's
jokes.explanation: This earns its high score through several layers. The full-name proposal ('Abbi Gary Anna Abrams') is immediately funny. Then both participants seem to immediately process the 'no' simultaneously — the call-and-response 'No. Right? / Right, yeah, no. / Yeah, no.' is a perfectly choreographed mutual recognition of absurdity. 'Two carats' as the only argument in favor is both Trey's character and a perfect joke about what men think proposals are about. 'It was a big swing' as his epitaph for the moment is excellent. Cringe comedy at its most precise.

jokes.text: Um... oh, Trey. Two carats. No. Right? Right, yeah, no. Yeah, no. You know. You know what? It was a big swing.
jokes.setup: Trey has just proposed; Abbi is processing
jokes.punchline: The negotiation of rejection plays out through a series of 'no' variations ('No. Right? Right, yeah, no. Yeah, no.') where both parties iteratively confirm the no while Trey reframes it as 'a big swing'
jokes.explanation: The choreography of the rejection — the mutual 'yeah, no' rhythm where both people are managing the awkwardness together — is excellent cringe comedy writing. 'It was a big swing' as Trey's philosophical acceptance shows genuine character depth under the comedy.

jokes.text: Maria... Cut the video, okay? Delete it. She said no.
jokes.setup: Trey's proposal has been rejected; he had someone filming it
jokes.punchline: He had video documentation of the proposal prepared — presumably for a yes — and calmly manages the footage in real time
jokes.explanation: A perfect tag to the proposal joke. The fact that Trey had a videographer named Maria standing by — at Abbi's going-away party — and the practical, businesslike 'Delete it. She said no' is both sad and hilarious. It reveals how seriously Trey took this and how thoroughly he had planned for it to work.

jokes.text: Okay, well, I should probably go 'cause you said no to my proposal, but promise me you'll eat for your blood type, okay?
jokes.setup: Trey prepares to leave after his rejection
jokes.punchline: He transitions from 'you rejected my marriage proposal' to 'eat for your blood type' as though these are equally weighted concerns — and the blood type diet is his parting wisdom
jokes.explanation: The logic pivot — 'I should probably go 'cause you said no to my proposal' — treats the rejection as a mundane administrative inconvenience rather than emotional devastation, and the immediate pivot to blood type diet is pure Trey. He's not hurt; he's a wellness bro who is already thinking ahead. The 'okay?' is perfectly earnest.

jokes.text: Thank you so much. All right, beefcake. You've had enough time to show off your rock-hard tits and your Adonis ass. It's time to leave and exit, dawg.
jokes.setup: Trey lingers after the rejection saying goodbyes
jokes.punchline: Ilana ejects him with a monologue that roasts his physique while simultaneously acknowledging it — 'rock-hard tits' and 'Adonis ass' are simultaneously compliments and dismissals
jokes.explanation: 'Leave and exit, dawg' — the redundancy of 'leave and exit' plus the unexpected 'dawg' — is Ilana's speech pattern at its best. 'Rock-hard tits' is specifically funny because it's an unusual descriptor for a man's chest. Ilana both objectifies and dismisses simultaneously, which is very on-brand.

jokes.text: And I took off work for this. / You did? That sucks. You know what else sucks? When your best friend moves away, and everybody's out for face time for her. Get out of here! But maybe we'll be friends. / I don't have any left.
jokes.setup: Trey protests being kicked out because he took work off
jokes.punchline: Ilana pivots to emotional honesty — losing a best friend — and then invites possible future friendship with Trey, who responds 'I don't have any left'
jokes.explanation: 'I don't have any left' — Trey's admission that he's out of friends — is unexpectedly dark and sad in a way that makes it funnier. It also explains his proposal: he's not just in love, he's lonely. The joke works on two levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: We're no longer dating, right? And, uh, we're gonna keep growing our real, yet platonic friendship. And that means honesty. So, um, I'm gonna be honest with you. You got to get the fuck out of here.
jokes.setup: Ilana is talking to Linc (her ex, Abbi's brother) at the party
jokes.punchline: Ilana builds a solemn framework of 'honesty in platonic friendship' before using it as a vehicle to tell him to leave — the honesty was purely instrumental
jokes.explanation: The structure here is very clean: establish philosophical framework → deploy it for completely mundane purpose. The formality of 'I'm gonna be honest with you' as a setup for 'get the fuck out' is a great misdirection. Character-specific because Ilana would absolutely use emotional honesty as a party-management tool.

jokes.text: I'm gonna be honest with you. You got to get the fuck out of here. / What? / Yeah, it's time to go.
jokes.setup: Ilana introduces 'honesty' as a virtue in a platonic friendship with Abbi's ex
jokes.punchline: The honest thing she needs to say is that he should leave — the setup of 'honesty' is a windup for the most basic possible sentiment
jokes.explanation: The preamble about honesty elevates what could have been a simple dismissal into a bit. It's quick and economical, which is its best quality.

jokes.text: You know, I know it's scary, you know, moving away. But it's good to remind ourselves that there's other places besides New York City. Beautiful places. And New Jersey.
jokes.setup: Someone is offering comfort about moving away from NYC
jokes.punchline: The inspirational thought that there are 'beautiful places' besides NYC is immediately qualified by 'And New Jersey' — implying New Jersey is either not beautiful or not a real place
jokes.explanation: Standard New Jersey joke. The 'And New Jersey' button is functional but well-trodden territory. Gets a mild laugh from the rhythm of the pause before it but doesn't bring anything new.

jokes.text: So, Ilana, who are all these people? / They're people from your building. I just needed to fill the party out.
jokes.setup: Abbi notices strangers at her going-away party
jokes.punchline: Ilana has simply invited building residents to pad attendance — she needed bodies and grabbed whoever was available
jokes.explanation: The honesty of 'I just needed to fill the party out' — no pretense, no social lubrication — is perfectly Ilana. It's also a gentle acknowledgment that maybe Abbi doesn't have that many people to invite, which is both funny and a little sweet.

jokes.text: So, Ilana, who are all these people? They're people from your building. I just needed to fill the party out.
jokes.setup: The party has many attendees Abbi doesn't recognize
jokes.punchline: Ilana recruited strangers from her building to pad out the guest list — with zero shame about this being a social strategy — and immediately starts ejecting these same fillers
jokes.explanation: 'I just needed to fill the party out' is delivered with complete normalcy, as if renting out strangers to give a going-away party volume is a standard social practice. The subsequent ejection of these guests is the logical follow-through.

jokes.text: Can't wait for brunch next weekend. Jersey City is pretty damn lit. / Mm-hmm. Yes, it is. / Go now, though.
jokes.setup: A stranger from the building makes a friendly comment about Jersey City to Ilana
jokes.punchline: Ilana agrees with Jersey City praise and immediately follows it with 'Go now, though' — no transition, no warmth
jokes.explanation: Ilana's ejection of party filler with the same energy she used on Trey — but now applied to a stranger she has no relationship with — is a quick well-executed beat. 'Jersey City is pretty damn lit. Go now, though.' is a funny non-sequitur pair.

jokes.text: Bevers's cheese storage gift — a labeled box so no one takes Abbi's cheese at her new place
jokes.setup: Bevers gives Abbi a going-away present
jokes.punchline: It's a personal cheese storage device — 'so you can store your cheese in here, and they won't be able to take it from you anymore' — revealing that cheese theft has been a real problem in Abbi's life (probably thanks to Bevers himself)
jokes.explanation: This is a deceptively rich joke. Bevers — who has presumably stolen Abbi's cheese throughout the series — gives her a cheese protection device as his parting gift, apparently without any self-awareness about his own role in the problem. 'They won't be able to take it from you anymore' is deeply funny because 'they' is almost certainly him. It's a perfect Bevers joke: generous, obtuse, and accidentally self-incriminating. The 'I love it. It's perfect.' from Abbi is a warm topper.

jokes.text: Ready? Disney. Go. / Euro-loving Aryan Nation pre-teen porn. Walt Disney was, like, um, jizzing, basically, for rich Euros and feudal lords, and Mickey Mouse was supposed to represent a dirty little Jew.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana settle in for what appears to be a late-night conspiracy theory game — one of them says a topic, the other riffs
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately delivers a fully-formed, deeply unhinged Disney antisemitism/conspiracy theory, apparently well-rehearsed
jokes.explanation: This is a very specific type of Broad City comedy — Ilana's Jewish identity intersecting with conspiratorial thinking in a way that somehow loops back to self-identification ('which I love to plot and scheme, so I'm like, you got me, Walt'). The joke is that Ilana has found a way to make a Disney antisemitism conspiracy theory personally affirming. It's transgressive in the exact way the show typically deploys transgression — through a Jewish character claiming the slur as empowering.

jokes.text: Ohh, the Kennedys. / Obviously, first of all, sex addicts. Second of all, they killed Marilyn Monroe.
jokes.setup: The conspiracy theory game continues; Abbi picks the Kennedys
jokes.punchline: Ilana has a fully organized Kennedy theory with numbered bullet points: sex addiction, then Marilyn Monroe murder
jokes.explanation: The organized numbering ('First of all... Second of all...') suggests Ilana has prepared Kennedy talking points. The joke is in the confidence and structure of her delivery of conspiracy material.

jokes.text: Wait, hold on. I thought Marilyn Monroe died of a drug overdose. / The government killed her because she knew too much. / Yeah, she knew about their bodies. She knew about their policies. / Like, where did you hear that? / I will double check, but, um... / It's widely known.
jokes.setup: Ilana claims the Kennedys killed Marilyn Monroe
jokes.punchline: 'It's widely known' — delivered with complete confidence about something she clearly hasn't verified — after 'I will double check' signals she has no source
jokes.explanation: 'I will double check, but, um... It's widely known.' is one of the best exchanges in the episode. The pause before 'It's widely known' — implying she's just thought of this defense — is perfect. The internal contradiction of 'I will double check' followed by 'widely known' (i.e., no checking needed) captures exactly how people defend bad information. Highly quotable, perfectly structured, strong character integration.

jokes.text: Well, these are gonna come in handy for when I'm lonely. / Okay, another one.
jokes.setup: Abbi is apparently referencing the conspiracy theory dolls or some object for companionship
jokes.punchline: 'For when I'm lonely' — a quiet admission that she'll be lonely in Boulder — delivered flatly and then immediately moved past with 'another one'
jokes.explanation: A small emotional undercurrent — Abbi acknowledging loneliness — played for a light laugh and then quickly redirected. Works because of the contrast between the subject matter (loneliness) and the tone (casual). The 'okay, another one' pivot is exactly right.

jokes.text: What's up? / The post office. / Oof. / Okay, so the way that the post office controls women is...
jokes.setup: Another round of the conspiracy game; Abbi picks the post office
jokes.punchline: 'The way that the post office controls women is...' — a cliffhanger that runs for over a minute of unheard content
jokes.explanation: The joke here is structural — we get the setup ('the way that the post office controls women is...') and then an entire minute-plus of Ilana's theory is withheld from us by a time cut. This is a brilliant comedy-of-omission: the joke is what we don't hear. The premise alone — Ilana has a fully-developed theory about the post office as a mechanism of female oppression — is peak character comedy, and withholding the content makes it funnier than any specific theory could be. 'Oof' from Abbi also suggests she knows what's coming and it's a lot.

jokes.text: Abbi? Abbi, Abbi, are you insane? You... you weren't gonna wake me up? Are you psychotic? You were gonna leave without saying goodbye?
jokes.setup: Abbi is apparently attempting to leave for Boulder without waking Ilana to say goodbye
jokes.punchline: Ilana's escalating series of accusations — 'insane,' 'psychotic' — for the offense of skipping a goodbye
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'insane' to 'psychotic' is funny because of the disproportionate psychiatric language applied to skipping a goodbye. It's also emotionally true — of course Ilana would react this way — which gives it texture beyond the joke.

jokes.text: I'm so proud of you. / I'm so proud of you, too, dude. / Really, you are, like... / You're the smartest person I know. / Really. / Yeah, are you kidding me? / I'm, like, the only person you know.
jokes.setup: Genuine mutual appreciation exchange at the final goodbye
jokes.punchline: Ilana undercuts Abbi's compliment — 'you're the smartest person I know' — with the observation that she may be Abbi's only friend
jokes.explanation: 'I'm, like, the only person you know' is a perfect comedic puncture of a sincere moment — funny because it might be true, and said by Ilana with casual honesty rather than cruelty. The joke works because it's both a roast and a love declaration: Ilana knows Abbi inside-out, including her limited social circle.

jokes.text: Come on, you dumb broads! / I'm saying goodbye to my best friend, you fucking fuck. / This is a once-in-a-lifetime, emotional moment with my soul sister, you rank rat whore! / See if I tip you!
jokes.setup: An emotional goodbye scene is interrupted by a cab driver or someone honking/rushing them
jokes.punchline: Ilana's full profanity escalation — 'you fucking fuck,' 'rank rat whore' — deployed in defense of sacred emotional time, ending with the threat of withheld tip
jokes.explanation: This is peak Ilana — protecting sacred friendship space with maximal profanity while the emotional scene continues uninterrupted. 'Rank rat whore' is an extraordinary insult construction, specific enough to feel invented on the spot. 'See if I tip you!' as the ultimate threat is perfectly New York. The joke works because Ilana doesn't pause the emotional register at all — she screams at the intruder and returns to 'You're just so beautiful from the inside out' without dropping a beat. The juxtaposition is the joke.

jokes.text: No! / Jesus, come on! / Fuck! [Ilana scrambling after Abbi's departing vehicle]
jokes.setup: Abbi has just left; the emotional goodbye is over
jokes.punchline: Ilana apparently chases the car briefly — the emotional overflow becoming physical comedy
jokes.explanation: The physical beat after the emotional peak — Ilana running after the car, swearing — is a classic comedy release valve after sustained sentiment. It's functional rather than brilliant, but it earns its place as an emotional-comedic transition.

jokes.text: Wow. / Oh, my God. / Yes! / Freaking jackpot. / Ew. / Eww, dude! Insane. Walking it? Disgusting.
jokes.setup: A post-credits or tag scene — Ilana apparently finds or uses the Wa-Unchi toilet
jokes.punchline: The toilet from the central plot returns — either Ilana has successfully installed it or discovers something new about it — and her reaction goes from 'jackpot' to 'disgusting' very quickly
jokes.explanation: A good callback to the day's central object — the toilet Ilana insisted was a cosmic mission. The reversal from triumph ('Freaking jackpot') to disgust ('Walking it? Disgusting') suggests the bidet function surprised her despite having described it in detail earlier. The tag is structurally satisfying as an episode closer.

jokes.text: Well, thank you for keeping me up to date on all of New York's most current events.
jokes.setup: Ilana is apparently describing the toilet/street incident to Abbi on the phone in post-series coda
jokes.punchline: Abbi's dry 'thank you for keeping me up to date on all of New York's most current events' — sarcastically framing Ilana's toilet dispatches as journalism
jokes.explanation: A nice grace note — the show's final season finale coda is them on the phone, and Abbi's sarcasm here captures exactly their dynamic: Ilana reports; Abbi receives. The irony of 'most current events' applied to toilet street finds is well-placed.

jokes.text: How have you been? / Well, since last night, I went to sleep, and then I woke up, and then I went to the coffee shop. / My coffee shop. / Cute, cute. / I also went to sleep and woke up, so we are on the same schedule.
jokes.setup: The post-finale coda — Abbi and Ilana are now in different cities catching up by phone
jokes.punchline: Their 'catching up' consists of sleep/wake cycles, and Ilana notes with satisfaction that they're on the same schedule — a deeply mundane shared identity
jokes.explanation: This is quietly one of the episode's warmer comedy moments — 'we are on the same schedule' applied to just sleeping and waking up is both funny and emotionally resonant. They've been separated but their biological clocks haven't changed. The joke captures the show's particular brand of absurd-but-true friendship observation.

jokes.text: Well, since last night, I went to sleep, and then I woke up, and then I went to the coffee shop. My coffee shop. I also went to sleep and woke up, so we are on the same schedule.
jokes.setup: Two people checking in with each other on their days
jokes.punchline: The 'update' is purely biological functions (sleep, wake, coffee) and 'my coffee shop' as a possessive claim over a public establishment, before noting the profound shared bond of both having slept and woken up
jokes.explanation: 'We are on the same schedule' as a profound connection derived from both having performed basic biological functions is a nice deadpan observation. 'My coffee shop' in passing is a good small character beat.

jokes.text: Ew, you know what happened to me today? I bumped into this girl, and I was, like, 'Sorry.' And she was, like, 'You're fine.' Ew. 'You're fine'? Fuck her. Right? I'm, like, I know I'm fine, at least. Just say, 'It's okay.' I mean, that's not even part of my vocabulary.
jokes.setup: Ilana recounts a sidewalk collision
jokes.punchline: Ilana is offended by 'You're fine' as a response to an apology — hearing it as a dismissal of her appearance rather than a social pleasantry — then adds that 'It's okay' is 'not part of her vocabulary' implying she also never says it
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp observational joke about language and social niceties that builds to a self-revealing punchline. The misread of 'you're fine' as 'you are fine [attractive]' is plausible enough to work, and 'that's not even part of my vocabulary' as a self-indicting admission (she wouldn't say 'it's okay' either) is excellent character work. 'I know I'm fine, at least' as her internal response to the perceived compliment/dismissal is very Ilana. Justifies 8+ on craft.

jokes.text: Ew, you know what happened to me today? I bumped into this girl, and I was, like, 'Sorry.' And she was, like, 'You're fine.' / Ew. 'You're fine'? / Fuck her. / Right? / I'm, like, I know I'm fine, at least. / Just say, 'It's okay.' / I mean, that's not even part of my vocabulary.
jokes.setup: Ilana recounts a minor social interaction
jokes.punchline: She's offended by 'You're fine' as a response to 'sorry' — interpreting it as a value judgment on her attractiveness rather than a social nicety — and the whole exchange escalates to 'Fuck her'
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant piece of New York micro-social observation. 'You're fine' as a passive-aggressive or thoughtless dismissal (vs. the warmer 'it's okay') is a real NYC phenomenon, and Ilana's instinct to reinterpret it as a personal attractiveness assessment — 'I know I'm fine, at least' — is perfect character work. It's the kind of specific, believable rage that feels lived-in. Abbi's immediate 'Fuck her' without needing any explanation is perfect best-friend comedy. This is the show at its strongest: tiny observation, big personality response.

jokes.text: I'm actually foiiine. / Oh, you foiiine, baby. / You, too.
jokes.setup: Continuing the 'you're fine' riff, Ilana reclaims the word
jokes.punchline: Both of them affirming each other's fineness in a mutual hype exchange that turns into its own small warm moment
jokes.explanation: A warm tag to the 'you're fine' exchange — the word gets reclaimed and turned into mutual affirmation. The elongated 'foiiine' spelling/pronunciation suggests a specific affectionate vocal performance between them. It's brief and character-specific.

jokes.text: Dude, I am so excited to fucking deejay this bat mitzvah tonight. Rich children, like, legit know how to party. / But honestly, like, what is deejaying? / I think it's just a playlist, right? / Yeah, but, like, with a table.
jokes.setup: Ilana has a bat mitzvah deejay gig
jokes.punchline: Their combined deconstruction of deejaying — 'just a playlist... but, like, with a table' — is a perfectly dumb-smart analysis
jokes.explanation: 'It's just a playlist, right?' / 'Yeah, but, like, with a table' is an extremely good exchange — it's wrong enough to be funny and right enough to be observational. The confidence in the table distinction is what makes it. Both characters arrive at this together, which is a strong friendship-comedy beat.

jokes.text: Dude, I am so excited to fucking deejay this bat mitzvah tonight. Rich children, like, legit know how to party. But honestly, like, what is deejaying? I think it's just a playlist, right? Yeah, but, like, with a table.
jokes.setup: Ilana announces she's deejaying a bat mitzvah
jokes.punchline: The deconstruction of deejaying to 'a playlist... with a table' strips away all mystique of the profession while being essentially correct
jokes.explanation: 'A playlist, but with a table' is a genuinely good observational joke — it's reductive, it's partially true, and it captures Ilana's complete confidence about an activity she clearly has no training in. 'Rich children legit know how to party' as a sincere observation is also good Ilana character voice.

jokes.text: You dropped these. / Oh, my God. Thanks.
jokes.setup: Ilana is walking and something drops
jokes.punchline: Whatever she dropped is apparently embarrassing enough for 'Oh, my God' — possibly something absurd given context — but the specific item is withheld (likely visible to the viewer, funny as a visual gag)
jokes.explanation: Without the visual, this is incomplete. The 'Oh, my God' implies something funny was dropped, but in transcript form this is unreadable. Scored conservatively — it may be funnier on screen.

jokes.text: Wow. Did you just make change with a homeless guy? / First of all, it's a man without a home. Second, we gonna act like he ain't got no small bills?
jokes.setup: Abbi witnesses Ilana making a financial transaction with a homeless man
jokes.punchline: Ilana first corrects the terminology ('man without a home') as a point of social justice, then defends the transaction on purely practical grounds — he has small bills, she needed change
jokes.explanation: This is the single best joke of the episode and among the sharpest the show has ever delivered. The two-beat structure is perfect: first Ilana performs progressive language correction ('man without a home'), which signals virtue — and then immediately follows it with a pragmatic non-apology that treats the transaction as completely normal ('we gonna act like he ain't got no small bills?'). The joke is about the gap between progressive signaling and actual behavior, executed in two sentences. It's original, economical, character-specific, and has a non-obvious punchline. Easily quotable. High rewatch value because the structure is so clean.

jokes.text: The information inside your brain is just vast. / Vast and then also, there's nothing. / Yeah, but good nothing. / You're, like, just smart, you know what I mean? / Am I, though? / Yes, you're so smart. / Thank you. / I wouldn't say at, like, you know, reading and writing, but...
jokes.setup: Abbi compliments Ilana's intelligence
jokes.punchline: 'I wouldn't say at, like, you know, reading and writing, but...' — Abbi qualifies the intelligence compliment by excluding literacy as one of Ilana's strengths
jokes.explanation: A perfect series-closing joke. The buildup — 'vast,' then 'vast and also nothing,' then 'good nothing,' then the confident 'you're just smart' — is a beautifully structured slow descent toward the qualifier. 'I wouldn't say at reading and writing' as an exception to 'smart' is a devastating and affectionate punchline. It's the last joke of the series and it lands with maximum character specificity: Abbi loves Ilana completely, including the parts that can't read good. The unfinished 'but...' suggests there are other exceptions she's tactfully leaving out, which is the real topper.
